title: Headache — Red Flags
oneLiner: Acute or severe headache with neurological or systemic features that threaten life or vision; primary goal is to rule out subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, and giant cell arteritis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH):** thunderclap headache (maximal at onset, instantaneous) + neck stiffness or GCS drop — do non-contrast CT head within 6 hours; if negative and high suspicion, proceed to LP.
- **Bacterial meningitis:** headache + fever + neck stiffness + altered mental state +/− non-blanching rash — start IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) immediately, *before* CT unless focal neurology or GCS <12.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA) with vision threat:** headache in patient >50y + jaw claudication + ESR/CRP markedly raised — high-dose IV methylprednisolone to prevent irreversible blindness.
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) with impending herniation:** headache + vomiting + drowsiness + papilloedema + Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) — immediate CT, then neurosurgery.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset (“thunderclap”) – worst ever, maximal at seconds/minutes
  - Progressive worsening over days to weeks (mass, venous sinus thrombosis)
  - Associated fever, neck stiffness, photophobia (meningitis)
  - Scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual disturbance (GCA)
  - Worsens with Valsalva, lying flat, or early morning (raised ICP)
  - Focal neurological symptoms (seizure, weakness, diplopia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Neck stiffness (meningeal irritation) – Kernig’s, Brudzinski’s signs
  - Fundoscopy: papilloedema, retinal haemorrhage (SAH), retinal artery occlusion (GCA)
  - Cranial nerve palsies (III, VI – raised ICP; III – posterior communicating aneurysm)
  - Fever, non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcal)
  - Blood pressure + pulse (Cushing’s reflex if raised ICP)
  - Temporal artery tenderness, pulselessness, nodularity (GCA)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Primary headache (migraine, tension, cluster)** – no red flags, normal exam, recurrent pattern.
  - **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** – thunderclap onset; CT + LP needed.
  - **Meningitis / encephalitis** – fever, neck stiffness, altered consciousness; LP diagnostic.
  - **Raised ICP (tumour, idiopathic intracranial hypertension)** – papilloedema, progressive, worse lying flat; imaging shows mass or empty sella.
  - **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache + seizure or focal signs in at-risk patient (OCP, dehydration, hypercoagulable); MRI/MRV.
  - **Giant cell arteritis** – age >50, raised ESR/CRP, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness – temporal artery biopsy.
  - **Acute angle-closure glaucoma** – red painful eye, fixed mid-dilated pupil, cloudy cornea – measure IOP.
  - **Pituitary apoplexy** – sudden headache + visual field defect (bitemporal hemianopia) + hypotension (adrenal crisis) – urgent MRI + endocrine.
- **Splitter:** *Presence of fever and neck stiffness* → meningitis vs SAH (LP can differentiate – CSF WCC vs RBCs). If thunderclap + no fever → SAH first.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - NEWS2, GCS
  - Fundoscopy (direct ophthalmoscopy – papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages)
  - Vital signs + temperature
  - If meningococcal suspected – do not wait for investigation; give antibiotics.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, ESR, coagulation screen, group & save
  - Blood cultures (2 sets) if fever
  - D-dimer (if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis suspected; sensitive but not specific)
  - CT head if SAH or mass suspected; LP after CT if SAH negative
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** non-contrast CT head (for SAH, mass, haemorrhage) – ideally within 1 hour of arrival, definitely within 6 hours of onset.
  - **Second-line:** CT venogram (if SVT); MRI brain with MRV (if SVT, mass, or atypical presentation); CT angiogram (if aneurysm suspected after negative non-contrast); MRI with contrast if suspected pituitary apoplexy or infection.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture (LP):** after CT if no mass or raised ICP; measure opening pressure, send for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, PCR (meningitis/encephalitis); also Xanthochromia (bilirubin) for SAH (avoid if traumatic tap).
  - **Temporal artery biopsy** (if GCA) – within 1 week of starting steroids; do not delay steroids.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **SAH:** Hunt and Hess grade (1-5) for prognosis – not for diagnosis.
  - **GCA:** ACR 1990 criteria – age≥50, new headache, temporal artery tenderness, ESR≥50, abnormal biopsy – 3 of 5 gives 94% sensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** **Non-contrast CT head** in any acute severe headache with features of SAH or raised ICP. If CT negative <6 hours of onset and high suspicion for SAH, **LP for xanthochromia** is the never-miss test (if no contraindication).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If meningitis suspected:** give IV ceftriaxone 2g stat (plus dexamethasone 10mg IV if bacterial meningitis). Do not delay for CT if no focal neurology and GCS≥12.
  - **If SAH suspected:** lie flat, IV fluids, analgesia (paracetamol, avoid NSAIDs if any suspicion of bleeding), urgent neurosurgical referral. Aim systolic BP <160 mmHg [check local policy for agent – e.g. labetalol or nimodipine after aneurysm securing].
  - **If GCA with vision threat:** IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3 days, then high-dose oral prednisolone (60mg). Immediate ophthalmology referral.
  - **If raised ICP with herniation signs:** elevate head of bed 30°, IV mannitol 0.5-1g/kg over 20 min or hypertonic saline; urgent neurosurgery.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **SAH:** maintain euvolemia, nimodipine 60mg 4-hourly to prevent vasospasm (if prescribed); monitor GCS, BP, sodium (SIADH/CSW).
  - **Meningitis:** continue antibiotics according to local policy (e.g. ceftriaxone, add amoxicillin if age >55 or immunocompromised for *Listeria*). Steroids (dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h for 4 days) if started before or with first antibiotic.
  - **GCA:** oral prednisolone 40-60mg daily, slow taper; GI protection (PPI), bone protection (calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonate if long-term). Refer rheumatology.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **GCA:** prednisolone taper over 12-18 months; monitor for relapse.
  - **Idiopathic intracranial hypertension:** weight loss, acetazolamide, follow-up neuro-ophthalmology.
  - **Post-dural puncture headache:** if occurred after LP – hydration, caffeine, blood patch.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** fluids 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h (monitor sodium in SAH), VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (only after aneurysm secured in SAH or after 48h in meningitis if no haemorrhage), diet as tolerated, observations: 1-hourly NEWS2 for first 4h then 4-hourly if stable. For raised ICP: strict input-output, GCS chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ceftriaxone 2g IV stat (meningitis) – then 2g IV 12h.
  - Dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h for 4 days (bacterial meningitis) – give before or with first antibiotic.
  - Nimodipine 60mg PO/NG 4-hourly for 21 days (SAH, after aneurysm secured) – monitor BP.
  - IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3 days (GCA with visual loss).
  - Prednisolone 40-60mg PO daily (GCA without vision loss, taper).
  - Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV over 20 minutes (acute raised ICP) – [check local policy for repeat dosing].
  - Paracetamol 1g IV/PO q6h (analgesia, safe in most).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav or cefotaxime if contraindication to ceftriaxone (e.g. severe penicillin allergy – seek microbiologist).
  - Acetazolamide 250-500mg PO bd (IIH).
  - Labetalol or nicardipine IV for BP control in SAH [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone – avoid with calcium-containing IV fluids in neonates (but not relevant here); caution in penicillin allergy (cross-reaction low but be aware).
  - Nimodipine – potentiate antihypertensives; avoid grapefruit juice; risk of hypotension.
  - High-dose steroids – increase blood glucose, risk of GI bleeding, infection; use PPI prophylaxis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Nimodipine:** no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose [check local policy].
  - **Ceftriaxone:** adjust if eGFR <10 (dose halved or interval extended – [check local policy]).
  - **Mannitol:** contraindicated in anuria, pulmonary oedema, acute tubular necrosis; use with caution in renal impairment – monitor osmolality.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Nimodipine:** giving first dose IV (it is only PO/NG); if accidentally given IV, severe hypotension and cardiac arrest.
  - **IV methylprednisolone** – rapid infusion over <20 minutes can cause arrhythmia; must be given slowly (e.g. over 30-60 min).
  - **Ceftriaxone** – failure to give before CT in suspected meningitis can delay treatment and worsen outcome; never hold for imaging unless signs of raised ICP.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - SAH: re-bleed, vasospasm (days 3-14), cerebral ischaemia, hydrocephalus, hyponatraemia.
  - Meningitis: sepsis, seizures, raised ICP, cerebral oedema, hearing loss.
  - GCA: irreversible visual loss (up to 20% if untreated), aortic dissection/aneurysm.
- **Chronic:**
  - SAH: cognitive deficits, chronic hydrocephalus (shunt-dependent), epilepsy.
  - Meningitis: neurological sequelae, hearing loss, learning difficulties.
  - GCA: steroid toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts), aortitis.
sections.Prognosis:
- SAH: mortality ~50% overall; survivors may have significant disability. Hunt & Hess grade predicts outcome (I/II good, IV/V poor).
- Bacterial meningitis: mortality ~10-15% (higher if pneumococcal, lower if meningococcal). Neurological sequelae in 20-30%.
- GCA: vision loss usually irreversible; with early high-dose steroids, risk of further visual loss drops to ~1-2%. Disease usually responds to steroids, but requires long-term taper.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check ESR + CRP + platelets** in any patient >50 with new headache – GCA urgencies can be missed.
- **In thunderclap headache with negative CT <6h, do not discharge without LP** – xanthochromia can be present for up to 2 weeks.
- **If meningitis suspected, give antibiotics within the first hour, even before blood cultures or CT** – this saves lives.
- **Document GCS and NEWS2 on every headache assessment** – deterioration may be first sign of herniation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume a benign headache because the patient is young** – SAH occurs in young too.
- **Do not give NSAIDs in suspected SAH** – they increase bleeding risk and mask symptoms.
- **Do not delay LP in SAH workup because of anticoagulation** – discuss with senior/haematology; the risk of missing SAH outweighs.
- **Do not start steroids in GCA without arranging immediate temporal artery biopsy** – steroids can render biopsy non-diagnostic within 2-3 days; but *do not* delay steroids for biopsy – biopsy can be done even after 1 week of steroids.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening headache; new neurological symptoms (weakness, speech, vision); fever; neck stiffness; vomiting; drowsiness.
- **Follow-up:** All patients with headache red flags who are discharged must have clear GP follow-up within 1 week. If suspected GCA or IIH, arrange same-day or next-day specialist review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - GCS ≤13 or drop by ≥2 points
  - Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)
  - New fixed dilated pupil (ipsilateral to mass – impending uncal herniation)
  - Seizure (new onset)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or increasing trend
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if septic)
  - Systolic BP >200 or <90 (SAH or sepsis)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Raised ICP:** GCS <12, pupillary asymmetry, loss of CT cisterns – immediate senior and neurosurgery.
  - **Lumbar puncture** – contraindicated if signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neurology, new seizure, GCS<12); seek senior advice.

rawMarkdown:
## Headache — Red Flags
**One-liner:** Acute or severe headache with neurological or systemic features that threaten life or vision; primary goal is to rule out subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, and giant cell arteritis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH):** thunderclap headache (maximal at onset, instantaneous) + neck stiffness or GCS drop — do non-contrast CT head within 6 hours; if negative and high suspicion, proceed to LP.
- **Bacterial meningitis:** headache + fever + neck stiffness + altered mental state +/− non-blanching rash — start IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) immediately, *before* CT unless focal neurology or GCS <12.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA) with vision threat:** headache in patient >50y + jaw claudication + ESR/CRP markedly raised — high-dose IV methylprednisolone to prevent irreversible blindness.
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) with impending herniation:** headache + vomiting + drowsiness + papilloedema + Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) — immediate CT, then neurosurgery.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset (“thunderclap”) – worst ever, maximal at seconds/minutes
  - Progressive worsening over days to weeks (mass, venous sinus thrombosis)
  - Associated fever, neck stiffness, photophobia (meningitis)
  - Scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual disturbance (GCA)
  - Worsens with Valsalva, lying flat, or early morning (raised ICP)
  - Focal neurological symptoms (seizure, weakness, diplopia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Neck stiffness (meningeal irritation) – Kernig’s, Brudzinski’s signs
  - Fundoscopy: papilloedema, retinal haemorrhage (SAH), retinal artery occlusion (GCA)
  - Cranial nerve palsies (III, VI – raised ICP; III – posterior communicating aneurysm)
  - Fever, non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcal)
  - Blood pressure + pulse (Cushing’s reflex if raised ICP)
  - Temporal artery tenderness, pulselessness, nodularity (GCA)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Primary headache (migraine, tension, cluster)** – no red flags, normal exam, recurrent pattern.
  - **Subarachnoid haemorrhage** – thunderclap onset; CT + LP needed.
  - **Meningitis / encephalitis** – fever, neck stiffness, altered consciousness; LP diagnostic.
  - **Raised ICP (tumour, idiopathic intracranial hypertension)** – papilloedema, progressive, worse lying flat; imaging shows mass or empty sella.
  - **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache + seizure or focal signs in at-risk patient (OCP, dehydration, hypercoagulable); MRI/MRV.
  - **Giant cell arteritis** – age >50, raised ESR/CRP, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness – temporal artery biopsy.
  - **Acute angle-closure glaucoma** – red painful eye, fixed mid-dilated pupil, cloudy cornea – measure IOP.
  - **Pituitary apoplexy** – sudden headache + visual field defect (bitemporal hemianopia) + hypotension (adrenal crisis) – urgent MRI + endocrine.
- **Splitter:** *Presence of fever and neck stiffness* → meningitis vs SAH (LP can differentiate – CSF WCC vs RBCs). If thunderclap + no fever → SAH first.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - NEWS2, GCS
  - Fundoscopy (direct ophthalmoscopy – papilloedema, retinal haemorrhages)
  - Vital signs + temperature
  - If meningococcal suspected – do not wait for investigation; give antibiotics.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, ESR, coagulation screen, group & save
  - Blood cultures (2 sets) if fever
  - D-dimer (if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis suspected; sensitive but not specific)
  - CT head if SAH or mass suspected; LP after CT if SAH negative
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** non-contrast CT head (for SAH, mass, haemorrhage) – ideally within 1 hour of arrival, definitely within 6 hours of onset.
  - **Second-line:** CT venogram (if SVT); MRI brain with MRV (if SVT, mass, or atypical presentation); CT angiogram (if aneurysm suspected after negative non-contrast); MRI with contrast if suspected pituitary apoplexy or infection.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture (LP):** after CT if no mass or raised ICP; measure opening pressure, send for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, PCR (meningitis/encephalitis); also Xanthochromia (bilirubin) for SAH (avoid if traumatic tap).
  - **Temporal artery biopsy** (if GCA) – within 1 week of starting steroids; do not delay steroids.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **SAH:** Hunt and Hess grade (1-5) for prognosis – not for diagnosis.
  - **GCA:** ACR 1990 criteria – age≥50, new headache, temporal artery tenderness, ESR≥50, abnormal biopsy – 3 of 5 gives 94% sensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** **Non-contrast CT head** in any acute severe headache with features of SAH or raised ICP. If CT negative <6 hours of onset and high suspicion for SAH, **LP for xanthochromia** is the never-miss test (if no contraindication).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If meningitis suspected:** give IV ceftriaxone 2g stat (plus dexamethasone 10mg IV if bacterial meningitis). Do not delay for CT if no focal neurology and GCS≥12.
  - **If SAH suspected:** lie flat, IV fluids, analgesia (paracetamol, avoid NSAIDs if any suspicion of bleeding), urgent neurosurgical referral. Aim systolic BP <160 mmHg [check local policy for agent – e.g. labetalol or nimodipine after aneurysm securing].
  - **If GCA with vision threat:** IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3 days, then high-dose oral prednisolone (60mg). Immediate ophthalmology referral.
  - **If raised ICP with herniation signs:** elevate head of bed 30°, IV mannitol 0.5-1g/kg over 20 min or hypertonic saline; urgent neurosurgery.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **SAH:** maintain euvolemia, nimodipine 60mg 4-hourly to prevent vasospasm (if prescribed); monitor GCS, BP, sodium (SIADH/CSW).
  - **Meningitis:** continue antibiotics according to local policy (e.g. ceftriaxone, add amoxicillin if age >55 or immunocompromised for *Listeria*). Steroids (dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h for 4 days) if started before or with first antibiotic.
  - **GCA:** oral prednisolone 40-60mg daily, slow taper; GI protection (PPI), bone protection (calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonate if long-term). Refer rheumatology.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **GCA:** prednisolone taper over 12-18 months; monitor for relapse.
  - **Idiopathic intracranial hypertension:** weight loss, acetazolamide, follow-up neuro-ophthalmology.
  - **Post-dural puncture headache:** if occurred after LP – hydration, caffeine, blood patch.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** fluids 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h (monitor sodium in SAH), VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (only after aneurysm secured in SAH or after 48h in meningitis if no haemorrhage), diet as tolerated, observations: 1-hourly NEWS2 for first 4h then 4-hourly if stable. For raised ICP: strict input-output, GCS chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ceftriaxone 2g IV stat (meningitis) – then 2g IV 12h.
  - Dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h for 4 days (bacterial meningitis) – give before or with first antibiotic.
  - Nimodipine 60mg PO/NG 4-hourly for 21 days (SAH, after aneurysm secured) – monitor BP.
  - IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3 days (GCA with visual loss).
  - Prednisolone 40-60mg PO daily (GCA without vision loss, taper).
  - Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV over 20 minutes (acute raised ICP) – [check local policy for repeat dosing].
  - Paracetamol 1g IV/PO q6h (analgesia, safe in most).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav or cefotaxime if contraindication to ceftriaxone (e.g. severe penicillin allergy – seek microbiologist).
  - Acetazolamide 250-500mg PO bd (IIH).
  - Labetalol or nicardipine IV for BP control in SAH [check local policy].
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone – avoid with calcium-containing IV fluids in neonates (but not relevant here); caution in penicillin allergy (cross-reaction low but be aware).
  - Nimodipine – potentiate antihypertensives; avoid grapefruit juice; risk of hypotension.
  - High-dose steroids – increase blood glucose, risk of GI bleeding, infection; use PPI prophylaxis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Nimodipine:** no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment – reduce dose [check local policy].
  - **Ceftriaxone:** adjust if eGFR <10 (dose halved or interval extended – [check local policy]).
  - **Mannitol:** contraindicated in anuria, pulmonary oedema, acute tubular necrosis; use with caution in renal impairment – monitor osmolality.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Nimodipine:** giving first dose IV (it is only PO/NG); if accidentally given IV, severe hypotension and cardiac arrest.
  - **IV methylprednisolone** – rapid infusion over <20 minutes can cause arrhythmia; must be given slowly (e.g. over 30-60 min).
  - **Ceftriaxone** – failure to give before CT in suspected meningitis can delay treatment and worsen outcome; never hold for imaging unless signs of raised ICP.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - SAH: re-bleed, vasospasm (days 3-14), cerebral ischaemia, hydrocephalus, hyponatraemia.
  - Meningitis: sepsis, seizures, raised ICP, cerebral oedema, hearing loss.
  - GCA: irreversible visual loss (up to 20% if untreated), aortic dissection/aneurysm.
- **Chronic:**
  - SAH: cognitive deficits, chronic hydrocephalus (shunt-dependent), epilepsy.
  - Meningitis: neurological sequelae, hearing loss, learning difficulties.
  - GCA: steroid toxicity (osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts), aortitis.

### Prognosis
- SAH: mortality ~50% overall; survivors may have significant disability. Hunt & Hess grade predicts outcome (I/II good, IV/V poor).
- Bacterial meningitis: mortality ~10-15% (higher if pneumococcal, lower if meningococcal). Neurological sequelae in 20-30%.
- GCA: vision loss usually irreversible; with early high-dose steroids, risk of further visual loss drops to ~1-2%. Disease usually responds to steroids, but requires long-term taper.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check ESR + CRP + platelets** in any patient >50 with new headache – GCA urgencies can be missed.
- **In thunderclap headache with negative CT <6h, do not discharge without LP** – xanthochromia can be present for up to 2 weeks.
- **If meningitis suspected, give antibiotics within the first hour, even before blood cultures or CT** – this saves lives.
- **Document GCS and NEWS2 on every headache assessment** – deterioration may be first sign of herniation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume a benign headache because the patient is young** – SAH occurs in young too.
- **Do not give NSAIDs in suspected SAH** – they increase bleeding risk and mask symptoms.
- **Do not delay LP in SAH workup because of anticoagulation** – discuss with senior/haematology; the risk of missing SAH outweighs.
- **Do not start steroids in GCA without arranging immediate temporal artery biopsy** – steroids can render biopsy non-diagnostic within 2-3 days; but *do not* delay steroids for biopsy – biopsy can be done even after 1 week of steroids.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening headache; new neurological symptoms (weakness, speech, vision); fever; neck stiffness; vomiting; drowsiness.
- **Follow-up:** All patients with headache red flags who are discharged must have clear GP follow-up within 1 week. If suspected GCA or IIH, arrange same-day or next-day specialist review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - GCS ≤13 or drop by ≥2 points
  - Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)
  - New fixed dilated pupil (ipsilateral to mass – impending uncal herniation)
  - Seizure (new onset)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or increasing trend
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if septic)
  - Systolic BP >200 or <90 (SAH or sepsis)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Raised ICP:** GCS <12, pupillary asymmetry, loss of CT cisterns – immediate senior and neurosurgery.
  - **Lumbar puncture** – contraindicated if signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neurology, new seizure, GCS<12); seek senior advice.
